title: Gastrolobium bracteolosum
taxonomy: Gastrolobium bracteolosum is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae, commonly known as the pea family. It belongs to the subfamily Faboideae and is further classified within the tribe Galegeae. This classification is based on the plant's morphological characteristics and genetic analysis. The tribe Galegeae is a group of legume plants that are commonly found in Australia, and Gastrolobium bracteolosum is one of the species within this group. Further classification within the species may be made at the level of subspecies or varieties, but this information is not readily available.
uses: Gastrolobium bracteolosum is a plant native to Western Australia, and its traditional uses include a potential source of medicine. However, there is limited research on its medicinal properties. Some studies suggest that the plant may have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, but further studies are needed to confirm its medicinal uses. The plant's seeds are said to contain toxic compounds, which may limit its potential for medicinal use. constituents: Gastrolobium bracteolosum contains a range of active constituents, including phenanthrene alkaloids and other glycosides. These compounds are responsible for the plant's toxicity and are primarily found in the leaves and stems. Research on the specific active constituents of Gastrolobium bracteolosum is ongoing, but it is known to be a highly toxic plant. side_effects: Gastrolobium bracteolosum, also known as poison spike or Christmas tree, is a highly toxic plant native to Western Australia. The plant contains toxic compounds called glycosides that can cause a range of symptoms if ingested. Side effects may include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain, and in severe cases, can lead to cardiac arrest and even death. There have been documented cases of livestock and humans being poisoned by the plant after ingesting its leaves or seeds. Due to its toxic nature, it's essential to handle Gastrolobium bracteolosum with caution and to educate others about its potential dangers.
